model_summary: Initial gVAR-ready observation model pairing six clinically framed cognitive‑state criteria (C01–C06) with six modifiable predictors (P01–P06). The criterion set targets dynamic, internally generated cognitive states (rapid thought flux, attention slippage, task-unrelated thought, perceived thought loudness, thought noise, and engagement) while predictors cover attention training, mindfulness, sleep regulation, behavioral activation, aerobic activity, and social-belonging supports. The plan emphasizes high within-person variance via frequent momentary EMA probes (4 prompts/day) plus a lightweight daily diary and passive streams to augment data richness. A compact predictor set minimizes redundancy and supports stable gVAR estimation while preserving the complaint’s resolution.
variable_selection_notes: Selected six criterion constructs (C01–C06) directly mapping the complaint’s core cognitive states: racing thoughts, sustained attention, passive-task mind-wandering, volitional thought quietness, internal mental noise, and engagement. Predictors include evidence‑aligned salutogenic levers with broad mechanism coverage: attention training (BIO), mindfulness (PSYCHO), sleep regulation (BIO), behavioral activation/CBT skills (PSYCHO), aerobic exercise (BIO), and social belonging/support (SOCIAL). Variables were chosen to maximize within-person variance and interpretability, while keeping collinearity manageable by avoiding multiple near-duplicate self-regulation constructs. Custom notes flag potential nonlinearities (e.g., social media) and emphasize per-person centering to stabilize gVAR estimates.

momentary_ema.notes: EMA prompts target C01–C06 and P01–P06; balance across dayparts to maximize within-person variance.

daily_diary.notes: Evening diary to capture sleep context, fatigue, and overall day experience.

passive_streams.notes: Incorporate passive sleep metrics and activity signals to complement EMA data without overburdening the user.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Baseline readiness: six predictors, six criteria; requires robust within-person centering and careful lag handling.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Favor continuous/near-continuous measurements (1–9 Likert or continuous time) to maximize within-person variance; avoid binary-only signals where possible.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Prompts may induce fatigue; implement optional late-prompt catch-ups and data-quality checks; impute cautiously at analysis stage.

safety_notes: Not a diagnosis. No medication dosing recommended. If acute risk or mania is detected, follow safety protocol and escalate to appropriate clinical care.

criteria_variables.label: High frequency of spontaneous thoughts (racing thoughts)

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate how many distinct thoughts you are experiencing in the last minute on a scale from 1 (very few) to 9 (very many).

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / bipolar and related disorders / other specified bipolar and related disorder / short-duration cyclothymic symptoms (<24 months) / racing_thoughts
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / bipolar and related disorders / other specified bipolar and related disorder / short-duration cyclothymic symptoms (<24 months) / *racing_thoughts
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: C01 reflects a high frequency of spontaneous thoughts labeled as racing_thoughts. Among candidates, idx 30074 corresponds to the leaf 'short-duration cyclothymic symptoms (<24 months) / racing_thoughts', which directly matches the variable concept of racing thoughts and is the most specific operational leaf available for this term. The EMA_self_report (4 prompts/day, 9-point Likert) aligns with capturing momentary frequency of racing thoughts, consistent with how C01 is intended in the pseudoprofile. Alternative leaves referencing flight_of_ideas imply more extreme or broader manic phenomena, which is less consistent with the described measurement context and intent.

The EMA self-report item asks the user to rate their current ability to sustain attention on one ongoing task (4 prompts/day, 9-point scale), aligning directly with a sustained attention operational construct. predictor_variables.label: Attention_Systems_Training (BIO)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Enhances top-down attentional control to reduce cognitive load and racing thoughts.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the last 6 hours, rate your engagement with attention training exercises on a 1–9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Higher attention training practice should reduce racing thoughts in near term.
Better attentional control should support sustained attention on tasks.
Enhanced attention regulation should reduce task-unrelated thoughts during passive tasks.
Improved cognitive control may increase perceived ability to quiet thoughts.
Attentional stabilization can reduce mental noise intensity.
Bottom-up clarity and task engagement may improve with focused training.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: The variable P01 is Attentional training in the BIO domain; among candidate leaves, none represent a BIO-level predictor of attention training. Most BIO leaves are EMA-based measurements (e.g., ema_attention_quality, attention_quality_rating) or non-BIO predictor concepts; thus no clear exact leaf match.
predictor_variables.unmapped_reason: No explicit BIO predictor leaf for Attention_Systems_Training or attention_training in the provided ontology leaf set. Closest matches are BIO EMA measurement leaves (e.g., ema_attention_quality / attention_quality_rating) or PSYCHO-domain attention interventions, which do not precisely map to the BIO predictor concept.

predictor_variables.label: Mindfulness_Based_Interventions (PSYCHO)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Cultivates nonjudgmental awareness of thoughts, reducing rumination and reactivity.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the last 6 hours, rate your mindfulness practice engagement on a 1–9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Mindfulness decouples cognitive noise, reducing racing thoughts.
Nonjudgmental awareness supports sustained attention.
Decentering to thoughts reduces task-unrelated thinking.
Awareness practice supports volitional quieting of thoughts.
Mindfulness can reduce perceived internal noise.
Mindfulness fosters engagement with present tasks.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable P02 Mindfulness_Based_Interventions (PSYCHO) maps to a mindfulness-based predictor. The most specific leaf match is open_monitoring_and_meta_awareness / noting_practice_basic (idx 3598), which directly represents a core mindfulness practice (noting/practice in open monitoring). Measurement mode is EMA_self_report with an item assessing engagement in mindfulness practice within the last 6 hours, aligning with the its measurement: rate your mindfulness practice engagement on a 1–9 scale. Mechanistically, mindfulness practice enhances meta-awareness and nonjudgmental monitoring of experiences, reducing rumination and reactivity—precisely the intended role of this predictor in the model context.

predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Regular circadian entrainment improves cognitive clarity and reduces cognitive noise at night/day transitions.

measurement.item_or_signal: Total nightly sleep duration (hours) from last night.

expected_effects.rationale: Longer or more regular sleep may reduce morning cognitive buzzing and racing thoughts.
Improved sleep supports daytime attention stability.
Better sleep reduces cognitive wandering during low-demand tasks.
Sleep regularity may increase perceived ability to quiet thoughts.
Sleep quality relates to perceived mental noise.
More restful sleep can improve daytime engagement.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable P03 maps to Sleep_Routines_and_Practices (BIO) with measurement focusing on total nightly sleep duration (hours) from last night via passive_daily_aggregate collected through Smartphone/Health app. Among candidates, the leaf Sleep_tracker_wearable (idx 6288) best matches an objective, device-based sleep duration signal within Sleep_devices_and_technologies / tracking_and_feedback. It provides a specific operational construct for passive sleep duration tracking, aligning with the measurement mode (passive) and channel (phone-integrated devices) and capturing the intended Sleep_Routines_and_Practices mechanism (circadian entrainment via consistent sleep-wake data) without overfitting to diary-based or subjective constructs.

predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Activates approach behaviors, reduces avoidance and cognitive load, potentially lowering racing thoughts and improving task engagement.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the last 6 hours, rate your engagement with activation/cognitive-behavioral tasks on a 1–9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Activation increases task focus and reduces racing thoughts.
Structured activities support sustained attention.
Engagement in activities reduces passive-task mind wandering.
Active strategies may aid quieting thoughts.
Engagement reduces internal cognitive noise.
Activation fosters daytime engagement.

The variable label Behavioral_Activation_and_CBT_Skills (PSYCHO), EMA-based measurement of engagement with activation/CBT tasks, and mechanism (activates approach behaviors, reduces avoidance, improves engagement) align with scheduling activation and CBT-skills practice. This is more specific than broader activation_design or monitoring leaves.

predictor_variables.label: Aerobic_Cardiorespiratory_Exercise (BIO)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Exercise improves cerebrovascular function and neurodynamic efficiency, supporting attention and reducing rumination.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Brisk walking, cycling, or other MVPA

measurement.item_or_signal: Daily minutes of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) detected by wearable.

expected_effects.rationale: Regular MVPA reduces cognitive buzzing and racing thoughts.
Aerobic activity supports attention regulation.
Physical activity can decrease mind-wandering during passive tasks.
Exercise-associated fatigue/relief can quiet intrusive thoughts.
Physical activity modulates mental noise via arousal stabilization.
Better energy and mood from exercise can boost engagement.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf is idx 5858: BIO > movement_physical_function_and_somatic_care > aerobic_and_cardiorespiratory_movement > continuous_locomotion > walking. The var P05 is Aerobic_Cardiorespiratory_Exercise (BIO) with measurement as Daily minutes of MVPA detected by wearable. Walking is a core, common form of continuous locomotion contributing to MVPA and is reliably captured by wearables, making this the most specific yet broadly representative operational construct among the BIO movement leaves. Other options are more modality-specific (e.g., cycling, dance), which would be less aligned with a general MVPA daily-minute metric. Mechanism rationale: aerobic activity improves cerebrovascular function and supports attention; measurement mode (passive wearable) and unit (minutes/day) align with MVPA recording.

Positive social context reduces task-unrelated thoughts.
Supportive contexts may bolster perceived control over thoughts.
Social connectedness can reduce subjective mental noise.
Belonging increases overall engagement with activities.

expected_collinearity_risks: Sleep-related predictors (P03) may covary with mood/activation predictors (P02, P04). Center per-person and consider orthogonalization if needed.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: Potential ceiling effects on 9-point scales for highly practiced skills; monitor distributions and apply transformations if needed.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Center/standardize predictors per participant; consider 1–2 hemilines for very high correlation predictors to reduce redundancy.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: If fatigue persists, consider temporarily dropping P06 in early analyses to reduce burden.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: Feasible with 6 predictors and 6 criteria; require careful centering and lag handling; 14 days of data collection is adequate for a stable initial gVAR estimation.

source_variable.label: High frequency of spontaneous thoughts (racing thoughts)

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate how many distinct thoughts you are experiencing in the last minute on a scale from 1 (very few) to 9 (very many).

criteria.label: Reduced sustained attention on a single activity
criteria.query_text_used: Reduced sustained attention on a single activity | Operational item/signal: Right now, rate your ability to sustain attention on one ongoing task (e.g., watching a show) on a 1–9 scale (1 = very poor, 9 = excellent). | Polarity: unknown

mapping.rationale: Variable C02 corresponds to the cognitive construct 'sustained attention' on a single activity. The EMA self-report item asks the user to rate their current ability to sustain attention on one ongoing task (4 prompts/day, 9-point scale), aligning directly with a sustained attention operational construct. Among candidates, idx 4334 is a NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL leaf explicitly labeled for sustained attention within a concentration/focused-attention cluster, making it the most specific and appropriate match for a general adult/EMA context as described in the pseudoprofile. This mapping avoids clinical deficit framing (e.g., sustained_attention_deficit) and aligns with the intended measurement modality and context.

source_variable.label: Reduced sustained attention on a single activity

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate your ability to sustain attention on one ongoing task (e.g., watching a show) on a 1–9 scale (1 = very poor, 9 = excellent).

criteria.label: High frequency of task-unrelated thoughts during passive tasks
criteria.query_text_used: High frequency of task-unrelated thoughts during passive tasks | Operational item/signal: Right now, rate the proportion of time during a low-demand/passive task that thoughts unrelated to the activity occupy (1 = none, 9 = all). | Polarity: unknown

mapping.rationale: C03 maps to high frequency of task-unrelated thoughts during passive tasks (passive-task mind-wandering). Among the candidate leaves, 1112 corresponds to sustained attention (attention_span / sustained_attention) and represents attentional maintenance with vulnerability to off-task thoughts during tasks, aligning directly with the EMA item that asks for the proportion of time thoughts unrelated to the activity occupy during a low-demand/passive task. This is the most specific operational construct among the options that captures the intended cognitive state (mind-wandering during passive tasks) given the EMA-based, momentary assessment. The pseudoprofile context supports this mapping by focusing on internal cognitive states and momentary variance, not on unrelated constructs like rumination or sleep-related impairment.

source_variable.label: High frequency of task-unrelated thoughts during passive tasks

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate the proportion of time during a low-demand/passive task that thoughts unrelated to the activity occupy (1 = none, 9 = all).

criteria.label: Perceived inability to volitionally quiet thoughts
criteria.query_text_used: Perceived inability to volitionally quiet thoughts | Operational item/signal: Right now, rate how easily you can quiet or stop the flow of thoughts (1 = very difficult, 9 = very easy). | Polarity: unknown

mapping.rationale: C04 maps to 'quietmind / thoughtquieting' leaf: the measurement item asks the respondent to rate how easily they can quiet or stop the flow of thoughts, measured via EMA_self_report on smartphone four times daily (sampling_per_day=4) with a 9-point Likert scale. This is the most specific, directly corresponding operational construct among the candidates (quietmind, thoughtquieting) and aligns with volitional thought quieting in the pseudoprofile's C04 description.

source_variable.label: Perceived inability to volitionally quiet thoughts

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate how easily you can quiet or stop the flow of thoughts (1 = very difficult, 9 = very easy).

criteria.label: Subjective internal mental 'noise' or buzzing
criteria.query_text_used: Subjective internal mental 'noise' or buzzing | Operational item/signal: Right now, rate the felt mental 'buzzing' or noise that interferes with thought clarity (1 = none, 9 = extreme). | Polarity: unknown

mapping.rationale: C05 corresponds to subjective internal mental 'noise' interfering with thought clarity. The candidate leaf 32050 describes rumination impairing concentration or task performance, which is a direct operationalization of internal cognitive noise disrupting momentary cognitive processing. Given the EMA_self_report, momentary assessment (4 prompts/day) of present mental noise aligns with this leaf as the best-specific match among candidates. Other leaves largely pertain to external noise, misophonia, or specific obsessive-compulsive phenomena, which are not as directly aligned with the internal, transient cognitive-noise construct of C05.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate the felt mental 'buzzing' or noise that interferes with thought clarity (1 = none, 9 = extreme).

criteria.label: Negative impact on enjoyment/engagement due to mental chatter
criteria.query_text_used: Negative impact on enjoyment/engagement due to mental chatter | Operational item/signal: Right now, rate how engaged you are with your current activity (e.g., watching a show) on a 1–9 scale (1 = not engaged at all, 9 = fully engaged). | Polarity: unknown

mapping.rationale: Variable C06 targets momentary engagement with ongoing activity measured via 4x daily EMA on a 9-point scale. The closest, most specific CRITERION leaf is taskengagement (engaging tasks) under NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL, which maps to engagement-based criteria and matches the measurement item (rate current engagement), channel (phone EMA), and mechanism (dynamic engagement during activity). This leaf best captures the intended engagement state in the six-criterion set.

source_variable.label: Negative impact on enjoyment/engagement due to mental chatter

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate how engaged you are with your current activity (e.g., watching a show) on a 1–9 scale (1 = not engaged at all, 9 = fully engaged).

predictors.label: Attention_Systems_Training (BIO)
predictors.query_text_used: Attention_Systems_Training (BIO) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Enhances top-down attentional control to reduce cognitive load and racing thoughts. | Intervention examples: Attention training exercises | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the last 6 hours, rate your engagement with attention training exercises on a 1–9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Attention_Systems_Training

mapping.rationale: The variable P01 is Attentional training in the BIO domain; among candidate leaves, none represent a BIO-level predictor of attention training. Most BIO leaves are EMA-based measurements (e.g., ema_attention_quality, attention_quality_rating) or non-BIO predictor concepts; thus no clear exact leaf match.
mapping.unmapped_reason: No explicit BIO predictor leaf for Attention_Systems_Training or attention_training in the provided ontology leaf set. Closest matches are BIO EMA measurement leaves (e.g., ema_attention_quality / attention_quality_rating) or PSYCHO-domain attention interventions, which do not precisely map to the BIO predictor concept.

source_variable.label: Attention_Systems_Training (BIO)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Enhances top-down attentional control to reduce cognitive load and racing thoughts.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the last 6 hours, rate your engagement with attention training exercises on a 1–9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Higher attention training practice should reduce racing thoughts in near term.
Better attentional control should support sustained attention on tasks.
Enhanced attention regulation should reduce task-unrelated thoughts during passive tasks.
Improved cognitive control may increase perceived ability to quiet thoughts.
Attentional stabilization can reduce mental noise intensity.
Bottom-up clarity and task engagement may improve with focused training.

predictors.label: Mindfulness_Based_Interventions (PSYCHO)
predictors.query_text_used: Mindfulness_Based_Interventions (PSYCHO) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Cultivates nonjudgmental awareness of thoughts, reducing rumination and reactivity. | Intervention examples: Mindfulness meditation, breath-focused practice | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the last 6 hours, rate your mindfulness practice engagement on a 1–9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Mindfulness_Based_Interventions

mapping.rationale: Variable P02 Mindfulness_Based_Interventions (PSYCHO) maps to a mindfulness-based predictor. The most specific leaf match is open_monitoring_and_meta_awareness / noting_practice_basic (idx 3598), which directly represents a core mindfulness practice (noting/practice in open monitoring). Measurement mode is EMA_self_report with an item assessing engagement in mindfulness practice within the last 6 hours, aligning with the its measurement: rate your mindfulness practice engagement on a 1–9 scale. Mechanistically, mindfulness practice enhances meta-awareness and nonjudgmental monitoring of experiences, reducing rumination and reactivity—precisely the intended role of this predictor in the model context.

source_variable.label: Mindfulness_Based_Interventions (PSYCHO)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Cultivates nonjudgmental awareness of thoughts, reducing rumination and reactivity.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the last 6 hours, rate your mindfulness practice engagement on a 1–9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Mindfulness decouples cognitive noise, reducing racing thoughts.
Nonjudgmental awareness supports sustained attention.
Decentering to thoughts reduces task-unrelated thinking.
Awareness practice supports volitional quieting of thoughts.
Mindfulness can reduce perceived internal noise.
Mindfulness fosters engagement with present tasks.

predictors.label: Sleep_Routines_and_Practices (BIO)
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep_Routines_and_Practices (BIO) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Regular circadian entrainment improves cognitive clarity and reduces cognitive noise at night/day transitions. | Intervention examples: Consistent bed/wake times | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Total nightly sleep duration (hours) from last night. | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Sleep_and_Circadian > Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: Variable P03 maps to Sleep_Routines_and_Practices (BIO) with measurement focusing on total nightly sleep duration (hours) from last night via passive_daily_aggregate collected through Smartphone/Health app. Among candidates, the leaf Sleep_tracker_wearable (idx 6288) best matches an objective, device-based sleep duration signal within Sleep_devices_and_technologies / tracking_and_feedback. It provides a specific operational construct for passive sleep duration tracking, aligning with the measurement mode (passive) and channel (phone-integrated devices) and capturing the intended Sleep_Routines_and_Practices mechanism (circadian entrainment via consistent sleep-wake data) without overfitting to diary-based or subjective constructs.

source_variable.label: Sleep_Routines_and_Practices (BIO)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Regular circadian entrainment improves cognitive clarity and reduces cognitive noise at night/day transitions.

measurement.item_or_signal: Total nightly sleep duration (hours) from last night.

expected_effects.rationale: Longer or more regular sleep may reduce morning cognitive buzzing and racing thoughts.
Improved sleep supports daytime attention stability.
Better sleep reduces cognitive wandering during low-demand tasks.
Sleep regularity may increase perceived ability to quiet thoughts.
Sleep quality relates to perceived mental noise.
More restful sleep can improve daytime engagement.

predictors.label: Behavioral_Activation_and_CBT_Skills (PSYCHO)
predictors.query_text_used: Behavioral_Activation_and_CBT_Skills (PSYCHO) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Activates approach behaviors, reduces avoidance and cognitive load, potentially lowering racing thoughts and improving task engagement. | Intervention examples: Activity scheduling, CBT skills practice | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the last 6 hours, rate your engagement with activation/cognitive-behavioral tasks on a 1–9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies > Behavioral_Activation_and_CBT_Skills

mapping.rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf maps directly to Behavioral Activation scheduling (behavioral_activation_scheduling) within PSYCHO / change_process_and_therapeutic_phases / skill_acquisition / behavioral_planning_and_execution_skills. The variable label Behavioral_Activation_and_CBT_Skills (PSYCHO), EMA-based measurement of engagement with activation/CBT tasks, and mechanism (activates approach behaviors, reduces avoidance, improves engagement) align with scheduling activation and CBT-skills practice. This is more specific than broader activation_design or monitoring leaves.

source_variable.label: Behavioral_Activation_and_CBT_Skills (PSYCHO)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Activates approach behaviors, reduces avoidance and cognitive load, potentially lowering racing thoughts and improving task engagement.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the last 6 hours, rate your engagement with activation/cognitive-behavioral tasks on a 1–9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Activation increases task focus and reduces racing thoughts.
Structured activities support sustained attention.
Engagement in activities reduces passive-task mind wandering.
Active strategies may aid quieting thoughts.
Engagement reduces internal cognitive noise.
Activation fosters daytime engagement.

predictors.label: Aerobic_Cardiorespiratory_Exercise (BIO)
predictors.query_text_used: Aerobic_Cardiorespiratory_Exercise (BIO) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Exercise improves cerebrovascular function and neurodynamic efficiency, supporting attention and reducing rumination. | Intervention examples: Brisk walking, cycling, or other MVPA | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Daily minutes of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) detected by wearable. | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Movement_and_Bodywork > Aerobic_Movement_Modalities

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is idx 5858: BIO > movement_physical_function_and_somatic_care > aerobic_and_cardiorespiratory_movement > continuous_locomotion > walking. The var P05 is Aerobic_Cardiorespiratory_Exercise (BIO) with measurement as Daily minutes of MVPA detected by wearable. Walking is a core, common form of continuous locomotion contributing to MVPA and is reliably captured by wearables, making this the most specific yet broadly representative operational construct among the BIO movement leaves. Other options are more modality-specific (e.g., cycling, dance), which would be less aligned with a general MVPA daily-minute metric. Mechanism rationale: aerobic activity improves cerebrovascular function and supports attention; measurement mode (passive wearable) and unit (minutes/day) align with MVPA recording.

source_variable.label: Aerobic_Cardiorespiratory_Exercise (BIO)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Exercise improves cerebrovascular function and neurodynamic efficiency, supporting attention and reducing rumination.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brisk walking, cycling, or other MVPA

measurement.item_or_signal: Daily minutes of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) detected by wearable.

expected_effects.rationale: Regular MVPA reduces cognitive buzzing and racing thoughts.
Aerobic activity supports attention regulation.
Physical activity can decrease mind-wandering during passive tasks.
Exercise-associated fatigue/relief can quiet intrusive thoughts.
Physical activity modulates mental noise via arousal stabilization.
Better energy and mood from exercise can boost engagement.

predictors.label: Structured_Social_Support_and_Belonging (SOCIAL)
predictors.query_text_used: Structured_Social_Support_and_Belonging (SOCIAL) | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Perceived support and belonging can buffer cognitive load and reduce rumination. | Intervention examples: Regular contact with supportive peers/family | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the last 6 hours, rate perceived social support and sense of belonging on a 1–9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [SOCIAL] > Social_Support_and_Belonging

mapping.rationale: The variable is Structured_Social_Support_and_Belonging (SOCIAL) with momentary EMA measurement of perceived social support and sense of belonging. Among candidates, idx 1405 is the most specific leaf under SOCIAL / social_support_and_belonging / network_building_and_maintenance / mapping_and_assessment / belonging_and_community_fit_assessment, aligning directly with belonging assessment. This best matches the measurement item (3–9 scale across a 6-hour recall) and the intended mechanism (social support/belonging buffering cognitive load and rumination).

source_variable.label: Structured_Social_Support_and_Belonging (SOCIAL)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Perceived support and belonging can buffer cognitive load and reduce rumination.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the last 6 hours, rate perceived social support and sense of belonging on a 1–9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Social support can dampen cognitive hyperactivity.
Belonging may stabilize attention via regulatory cues.
Positive social context reduces task-unrelated thoughts.
Supportive contexts may bolster perceived control over thoughts.
Social connectedness can reduce subjective mental noise.
Belonging increases overall engagement with activities.
